q: Electrical energy is energy associated with the flow of charge through any part of a circuit.
Electrical energy can be generated from photovoltaic cells.
Electrical energy can be stored for future application using fuel cells and capacitors.
The work done $W$ in bringing the amount of charge $Q$ from point A to B is $W = QV$.
Potential difference is defined as the work done in bringing a unit positive charge from one point to another.
Electrical energy supplied by a battery is transformed into heat energy because electrons collide with the atoms in the resistor.
When electrons collide with atoms in a resistor, the atoms acquire additional energy and produce heat energy.
The electrical energy $W$ produced by a battery is given by $W = VIt = I^2 Rt = \frac{V^2}{R}t$.
An electric motor transforms electrical energy into heat energy instead of mechanical energy.
The practical unit of electrical energy is the kilowatt hour ($kWh$).
One kilowatt hour is equal to $3.60 \times 10^6\ J$.
Electrical power is the rate of transfer of electrical energy.
Electrical power $P$ is expressed as $P = \frac{W}{t}$.
The formulas for electrical power are $P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$.
The SI unit of electrical power $P$ is the watt ($W$).
One watt is equivalent to $1\ joule\ per\ second$ ($J\ s^{-1}$).
One unit of electricity is defined as $1\ kWh$.
Electrical energy cannot be stored in inductors.
If an electric lamp is connected in a circuit, it converts electrical energy into heat and light energy.
The relation $1\ kWh = 1000\ W \times 1\ h$ is used to convert $kWh$ to joules.
Work $W$ by a battery taking charge $Q$ from A to B is equivalent to the electrical energy supplied by the battery.
Electrical energy can be generated from dynamos and generators.
